data.title: Malaysia's Employees Provident Fund seeks more diverse exposure
data.description:
Malaysia's largest state pension fund, the Employees Provident Fund, is seeking to expand its exposure to other regions to ensure a well-balanced and resilient portfolio, it said on Wednesday.

EPF, one of the world's biggest pension funds, in common with other major investors is grappling with the uncertainty triggered by U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs and policy orders.

In an emailed statement, the fund said the U.S. remained a critical partner in global trade. Together with China, it said, the U.S. was central to its globally diversified portfolio because of the size, liquidity and global influence of the two markets.

It did not give detail on how it was seeking to diversify its holdings.

As of end-2024, the EFP had 1.25 trillion ringgits ($283 billion) of investment and its global assets generated 50.3% of its total investment income, while making up 37% of its total porfolio, according to a statement in March.

Domestic made up 63% of its investment, generating 49.7% of its total investment income.

($1 = 4.4160 ringgit)

data.title: Italy Inflation Rate Revised Lower
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Italy rose to 1.9% in March of 2025 from 1.6% in the earlier month, the highest in 18 months, but revised lower from the preliminary estimate of 2%.

The increase marked the continued stabilization of price growth among major parts of the Italian consumer basket that underwent periods of surges and plunges amid energy and food shocks after the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

Non-regulated energy prices rebounded (0.7% vs -1.9% in February), while they rose at a faster pace for tobacco (4.6% vs 4.1%), non-processed food (3.3% vs 2.9%), and recreational and cultural services (3.5% vs 3.1%).

In turn, regulated energy inflation slowed (27.2% vs 31.4%).

Annual core inflation, which excludes energy and fresh good, was unchanged from the previous month at 1.7%.

From the previous month, the CPI rose by 0.2%.

data.title: Altius Telecom Infra Trust to issue 10-year bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's Altius Telecom Infrastructure Trust plans to raise 12 billion rupees ($140.09 million) through the sale of bonds maturing in 10 years, three bankers said on Wednesday.

The trust will pay a coupon of 7.45%, which would be payable on a quarterly basis and has invited bids from bankers and investors for the issue on Thursday, they said.

The company did not reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.

data.title: India's Headline Inflation Eases to 3.3% in March, Lowest Since August 2019
data.description:
India's retail inflation eased to 3.3% in March from 3.6% a month earlier, the lowest level since August 2019, government data showed on Tuesday.

Food inflation dropped sharply to 2.7% from 3.8%, driven by lower prices for vegetables, eggs, pulses, meat, cereals, and milk.

Rural inflation fell to 3.3% from 3.8%, while urban inflation edged up to 3.4% from 3.3%. Food inflation in rural areas eased to 2.8% from 4.1%, and in urban areas to 2.5% from 3.2%.

Fuel and light inflation turned positive at 1.5% after contracting 1.3% in February. Housing inflation rose slightly to 3%, and education and health inflation climbed to 4% and 4.3%, respectively.

Transport and communication costs increased 3.3%, up from 2.9%.

Coconut oil, coconut, gold, silver, and grapes recorded the steepest year-on-year price gains, while ginger, tomato, cauliflower, jeera, and garlic saw the sharpest declines.

With inflation easing, the RBI has adopted an accommodative stance, cutting its key policy rate twice in 2025.

Economists anticipate further rate cuts, potentially two to three more within the year, to support economic growth. The RBI projects CPI inflation to average 4% for the fiscal year, assuming normal monsoon conditions.

data.title: Japan Orders Google to Stop Anticompetitive Smartphone App Practices
data.description:
The Japan Fair Trade Commission has directed Google to halt its practice of forcing smartphone manufacturers to preinstall its search and browser apps, according to a notice on Tuesday.

The antitrust regulator said such a requirement violates competition laws, and that Google's dominance in the search market unfairly restricts competition.

This follows the commission's investigation and collaboration that was initiated on Oct. 23, 2023. In the process, the regulator collaborated with foreign competition authorities who were also examining similar conduct by Google.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Wednesday at 4 AM ET
data.description:
IEA Cuts Oil Demand Forecast as U.S. Tariffs Weigh on Economic Growth

Oil demand growth is forecast to be weaker than previously projected as escalating global trade tensions sparked by U.S. President Trump's tariffs weigh on the global economy, the International Energy Agency said.

Europe's Appetite for Wind Power Helps Sandbrook Capital Find an Exit

Sandbrook Capital scored its first exit, a roughly $1 billion deal for a Norwegian service provider for offshore-wind-energy project developers, benefitting from the sector's fast expansion in Europe even as it fell into disfavor in the U.S.

Oil Futures Edge Up in Choppy Session

Crude futures rose in an up-and-down session, with U.S. tariff relief on electronic goods helping to push up prices. WTI settled up 3 cents at $61.53 a barrel, and Brent rose 0.2% to $64.88 a barrel.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on TotalEnergies, BP, Viva Energy, and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

OPEC Cuts Oil Demand Outlook Amid U.S. Tariff Hit

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries cut its forecast for oil demand growth just days after a surprise decision to boost output, citing the impact of U.S. tariffs on the global economy and crude consumption.

BP Discovers Oil at U.S. Far South Prospect in Gulf of Mexico

Initial data shows high-quality reservoirs and a potentially commercial volume of hydrocarbons, the company said.

Trump Has a $350 Billion Deal for Europe: Buy Our Energy

The continent has sharply increased its U.S. purchases in recent years, with little appetite to buy more.

Nuclear Power Is Back. And This Time, AI Can Help Manage the Reactors.

Argonne National Lab has an AI-based tool that can help design and operate nuclear reactors-at a time when AI itself is feeding a power frenzy.

BP Expects First-Quarter Upstream Production to Fall

The company anticipates lower upstream production than the previous quarter due to already announced divestments in Egypt and Trinidad.

Trump Promised a Golden Age for Oil and Gas, but Tariffs Undermine His Pledge

Fractured trade relations and a potential global recession are poised to reduce international sales and domestic output of fossil fuels.

data.title: Market Chatter: KKR-Backed India Lender Hopes to Raise $175.1 Million for Private Credit Fund
data.description:
KKR-backed lender InCred Financial Services' credit arm hopes to raise 15 billion Indian rupees ($175.1 million) for a new fund aimed at India's demand for private credit, Bloomberg News reported, citing an interview with Saurabh Jhalaria, the group's chief investment officer.

InCred Alternative Investments plans to lend to small and medium-size companies, especially those backed by institutional investors, the report said.

"We are looking for an internal rate of return around 16% to 17% for our performing credit funds," Bloomberg quoted Jhalaria as saying.

data.title: ASML Logs Weak Orders, Warns on Tariff Uncertainty3rd Update
data.description:
By Mauro Orru

ASML Holding posted orders below analysts' projections for the first quarter as chip makers held back spending on its semiconductor-making machinery, and warned that U.S. President Trump's erratic tariff policies were creating uncertainty for the industry.

The Dutch company reported 3.94 billion euros ($4.45 billion) in orders, up from 3.61 billion euros a year earlier, but below analysts' forecast of 4.84 billion euros, according to consensus estimates by Visible Alpha.

ASML, which counts Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. and Samsung Electronics among its clients, said 1.2 billion euros in orders were for its high-end extreme ultraviolet lithography systems that let semiconductor manufacturers print the most intricate layers on chips. Analysts had forecast 1.58 billion euros in orders for those EUV machines.

ASML's orders can fluctuate significantly between quarters as they are heavily influenced by how much chip makers are willing to spend based on the demand trends they see. The company said earlier this year that it would stop reporting quarterly orders as of 2026 because it believes the metric doesn't necessarily reflect business momentum.

The weak bookings came after DeepSeek developed artificial-intelligence models that it said nearly matched American rivals despite using inferior chips. The revelation from the Chinese AI company back in January sparked a market selloff as investors questioned whether businesses really need to spend huge sums on advanced gear to develop powerful AI models.

ASML Chief Executive Christophe Fouquet said AI continued to be the primary growth driver in the industry. However, lower-than-expected orders for the quarter show that chip makers were hesitant to invest in ASML's sophisticated equipment as appetite for legacy chips found in cars, smartphones, laptops and other devices remains sluggish.

The company in October slashed its sales forecasts for 2025, saying some areas of the semiconductor industry aside from AI were taking longer than expected to recover. ASML is forecasting between 30 billion and 35 billion euros in sales, a range it expects to grow to roughly 44 billion to 60 billion euros in 2030.

Fouquet said the company could hit the upper end of guidance this year if AI demand remains strong, though he cautioned that uncertainty with some customers could also take ASML to the lower end of guidance.

ASML shares in Amsterdam fell 6% in early morning trade. The stock is down more than 35% over the past 12 months as the company has been navigating the uneven demand.

Now, the industry faces a reckoning with Trump's erratic tariff policies that could weigh on its recovery. Smartphones, computers, memory chips and several other categories of products will be exempt from tariffs imposed earlier this month.

However, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said many tech products will still face separate levies in a month or two as part of an investigation into semiconductors.

"Our conversations so far with customers support our expectation that 2025 and 2026 will be growth years," Fouquet said in a statement. "However, the recent tariff announcements have increased uncertainty in the macro environment and the situation will remain dynamic for a while."

Chief Financial Officer Roger Dassen said tariffs could hit ASML and the wider semiconductor industry in different ways if Washington were to impose levies on equipment or parts and tools that the company imports into the U.S. Third countries slapping tariffs on what ASML ships from the U.S., where it has some manufacturing, is also a scenario that the company is considering, Dassen said.

"We're very actively working with the entire ecosystem to try and minimize the overall impact," Dassen said. "But clearly our intent is that the impact that it should have on our financials should be as limited as possible."

ASML reported sales of 7.74 billion euros for the first quarter, up from 5.29 billion euros a year earlier. The figure is slightly shy of analysts' forecasts, but within company guidance.

Net profit increased to nearly 2.36 billion euros from 1.22 billion euros, beating market expectations. Gross profit--a closely watched metric for companies operating in the semiconductor industry--came in at 4.18 billion euros, generating a 54% margin that beat both consensus and company guidance.

For the current quarter, the company expects sales at 7.2 billion to 7.7 billion euros and a gross margin between 50% and 53%, a range it said was wider than usual due to uncertainty around tariffs.

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: create restaurants' Profit Jumps 11% in Fiscal 2025
data.description:
create restaurants holdings reported an 11% rise in profit attributable to owners of the parent to 5.59 billion yen for the fiscal year ended Feb. 28, from 5.04 billion yen a year ago.

The restaurant chain operator's basic earnings per share rose to 26.57 yen from 23.97 yen a year ago, according to a financial results filing.

Revenue jumped 7.3% to 156.4 billion yen from 145.8 billion yen a year earlier.

For the fiscal year ending February 2026, the company expects attributable profit to increase 3.7% to 5.80 billion yen or 27.57 yen per share, while revenue is expected to rise 5.5% to 165 billion yen.

The company expects to pay interim and year-end dividends of 4.50 yen per share, each, for the current fiscal year.

It declared a final dividend of 4 yen per share for fiscal 2025, payable from May 14.

data.title: Majid Al Futtaim invests $1.4 billion to expand Dubai's Mall of the Emirates
data.description:
Majid Al Futtaim plans to invest 5 billion dirhams ($1.36 billion) to transform the Mall of the Emirates into what it describes as a next-generation lifestyle destination to mark the mall's 20th anniversary.

The project will add 20,000 square metres of retail space, introducing 100 stores and new food, cultural and wellness area, the Emirati retail conglomerate said.

It has already allocated 1.1 billion dirhams to a wellness club, cultural hub, dining precinct and infrastructure improvements.

Dubai, home to the world's tallest tower and famous palm-shaped islands, has tourism as a cornerstone of its economy and destination malls like Mall of the Emirates are increasingly doubling as entertainment and cultural hubs that serve as key attractions for the city's international visitors.

The emirate is also seeking to triple the exhibitions and event management sector's annual contribution to GDP to $14.7 billion by 2033.

Majid Al Futtaim will also launch four new entertainment offerings by late 2026, it said without providing further detail.

($1 = 3.6728 UAE dirham)

Gold Futures Rise to New High on Safe-Haven Demand Amid Widening Trade WarMarket Talk
data.description: Gold futures rise to a new record high, with investors fleeing to safe-haven assets as Asian equity markets follow U.S. stock futures lower. The benchmark Hang Seng Index was down 2.5% at midday in Hong Kong, with the market waiting for uncertainty around U.S. President Trump's trade policies to clear up. The U.S. Commerce Department said it has initiated probes into the national security effects of importing semiconductors and pharmaceutical imports. The safe-haven demand for gold from the prospect of a wider trade war was accentuated by Chinese purchases, where gold-backed ETFs are picking up in demand, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. Futures are up 2.3% at $3,315.90 a troy ounce, having peaked at $3,317.80/oz earlier in the session. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0810 GMT - Gold futures rise to a new record high, with investors fleeing to safe-haven assets as Asian equity markets follow U.S. stock futures lower. The benchmark Hang Seng Index was down 2.5% at midday in Hong Kong, with the market waiting for uncertainty around U.S. President Trump's trade policies to clear up. The U.S. Commerce Department said it has initiated probes into the national security effects of importing semiconductors and pharmaceutical imports. The safe-haven demand for gold from the prospect of a wider trade war was accentuated by Chinese purchases, where gold-backed ETFs are picking up in demand, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. Futures are up 2.3% at $3,315.90 a troy ounce, having peaked at $3,317.80/oz earlier in the session. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0804 GMT - Investors turn negative on the outlook for U.S. corporate profits, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey says. A net 28% of responding investors say that the outlook for U.S. profits is unfavorable, the survey says. This is the highest print for this indicator since November 2007. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0756 GMT - Bets on a weaker dollar rose to their highest level in nearly 19 years in April, according to the latest Bank of America global fund manager survey. Some 61% of investors expect a weaker dollar in the next 12 months, the most since May 2006. The survey also showed 69% of investors see the dollar as overvalued, up 10 percentage points from the previous month and the highest since December 2022. The survey comes in the wake of President Trump's decision to impose sweeping tariffs against U.S. trading partners. This has weighed on the dollar due to concerns about the impact on U.S. economic growth. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0754 GMT - Heineken delivered another quarter of positive signs in key growth engines such as Vietnam, Mexico, Brazil and India, Jefferies analysts Edward Mundy and Andrei Andon-Ionita say in a note. The Dutch brewer is currently at an early stage of a multi-year turnaround program made to deliver profitable growth and improving free cash flow, they say. Heineken backed its guidance for 2025 for growth in both volume and revenue, and net profit growth in line with operating profit growth. On the negative side, Heineken unperformed in Europe, but this is likely to improve into the second quarter and face easier comparatives in the third quarter, they add. "As confidence in Heineken's compounding potential increases, shares should re-rate," the analysts add. Shares are up 2.1% at 76.74 euros.(michael.susin@wsj.com)

0751 GMT - Italy's opinion on whether to use its so-called golden powers to allow UniCredit's takeover of smaller peer Banco BPM could come earlier than expected and with conditions, Equita says in a research note after two Italian newspapers reported the decision could land in the next few days. A conditional approval is reportedly likely, with limits on branch reductions and on the rationalization of investment banking activities, guarantees on its headquarters' location and a potential exit from Russia. "These elements do not, in our opinion, diminish the industrial sense of the deal; however, it will be necessary to understand their magnitude in detail in order to properly assess the impact on the generation of synergies and value creation from the transaction," analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - Global equity allocation is at the lowest level in 21 months, the Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Allocation to global equities stood at a net 17% underweight in April, the most underweight since July 2023, BofA says. Allocation to stocks has declined 52 percentage points since February, marking the biggest 2-month drop since April 2020. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0743 GMT - The euro could rise further if the latest eurozone balance of payments data add to the story of a rotation out of U.S. equities into Europe, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. The data could show investments in non-eurozone equities by eurozone residents fell if they are repatriating, they say. It could also show foreigners bought more eurozone equities if they are increasing their exposure to Europe, helped by fiscal stimulus plans in the region. "If the above turns out to be true, investors should have a little more faith in the euro-dollar rally." The euro rises 0.7% to $1.1364 and ING expects it to test $1.15. The data are due at 0800 GMT. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0742 GMT - Hays demonstrates upside potential in the longer term, despite current slowdown in activity, RBC Capital Markets analysts Karl Green and Andrew Brooke say in a research note. The U.K. staffing company posted an in-line third quarter and gave an outlook that is as reassuring as it can currently get, the analysts say. "We think the through-cycle earnings power of the business will eventually come back into focus when geopolitical tensions start to ease," they say. Shares trade 0.1% lower at 69.75 pence. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0736 GMT - Chinese shares ended mixed. The Shanghai Composite Index gained 0.3%, the Shenzhen Composite Index fell 1.1% and the ChiNext Price Index declined by 1.2%. Any developments in the trade tensions between U.S. and China remain in focus. Among the major stocks, semiconductor companies led the gains. Hygon Information Technology added 2.7% and SMIC rose 1.8% after Nvidia said it expected to take up a charge of about US$5.5 billion associated with its AI H20 chips for the first quarter. Decliners included BYD, which dropped 2.2% and CATL, which closed 1.4% lower. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0724 GMT - Barratt Redrow's update is dull and that is probably a good thing given the global market turmoil backdrop, Jefferies analysts say in a note. The company backed completions guidance for FY 2025,with its order book benefiting from house-price inflation as well as mix, Jefferies says. Still, the FTSE 100 listed house builder's slightly improving selling rates might fail to excite, with lower affordable sales continuing to constrain order book progression, Jefferies says. "Growth in the value of the private order book and evidence of house-price inflation in the order book should reassure of the robustness of customer demand," Jefferies says. Shares are up 4.40 pence, or 1%, at 432.10 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0715 GMT - Antofagasta's first-quarter copper production was 2% lower than expected due to a weaker performance at its Los Pelambres mine, which was impacted by a national power outage and maintenance, RBC Capital Markets analysts Ben Davis and Marina Calero write. Its average copper price $4.69 a pound was 13% higher than expected, they write. Antofagasta's permit for its Zaldivar mine is due to expire in May and its renewal is going to the wire, however, there is no indications that it shouldn't be received in time, they add. Shares fall 2.2% at 1,502 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0706 GMT - Heineken's performance, which beat expectations in the first quarter, was equally driven by sales volume and price, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a note. While the Dutch brewer outperformed in Africa and Middle East, the most significant performance came from Vietnam, which along with India and Ethiopia delivered "promising volume growth," they say. Heineken delivered midteens organic volume and revenue growth in Vietnam, beating market expectations. Meanwhile, the board maintained its trend of prudent expectations, which became evident in recent quarters, they say. Shares rise 2.2%.(michael.susin@wsj.com)

data.title: Bank of England Can Keep Cutting Rates if Services Inflation Eases FurtherMarket Talk
data.description: Cooler inflation in the U.K. services sector should ensure the Bank of England cuts interest rates at a steady pace this year, ING economist James Smith writes in a note to clients. Headline annual inflation slowed to 2.6% in March, figures show Wednesday, while in services, annual inflation fell for the first time this year, to 4.7% from 5.0% previously. That's still uncomfortably high for BOE rate setters, Smith says. But the rate should continue to ease over the coming months, taking it lower than the central bank has forecast. "More benign services inflation, if it materialises ... would help cement one rate cut per quarter for the rest of 2025 and into 2026," Smith says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: LearnWorlds hits 500,000 courses milestone, signaling demand for a new generation of elearning platforms
data.description:
The milestone reflects global momentum for LearnWorlds’ unique blend of LMS power and course platform agility, trusted by over 11,000 customers worldwide

ATHENS, Greece, April 16, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — LearnWorlds, the elearning platform that combines the power of an LMS with the agility of a modern course builder, has officially surpassed 500,000 courses created by its users.

The milestone comes shortly after another major achievement: LearnWorlds customers have generated over $1 billion in revenue, driven by businesses responding to a shift in learner expectations for more flexible, relevant, and self-directed educational experiences.

Together, these achievements underscore LearnWorlds’ role as a trusted partner for creators, businesses, and educators building scalable learning experiences. With more than 11,000 customers in 150 countries, including global brands like Booking.com, Wagamama, and Yamaha, LearnWorlds is powering a new era of professional training, customer education, and knowledge monetisation.

Talking about the news, LearnWorlds’ CEO Panos Siozos said:

“We built LearnWorlds to give people the best of both worlds: enterprise-grade learning infrastructure and intuitive tools for creating and selling content. Reaching 500,000 courses is not just a number—it reflects a fundamental shift in how knowledge is delivered and consumed. Businesses and creators are no longer choosing between flexibility and scale—they’re demanding both. And platforms like ours are rising to meet that expectation.”

The milestone reflects not just growth, but the platform’s evolution. LearnWorlds has continued to expand its capabilities, from localisation and no-code mobile learning apps to AI-powered instructional intelligence, equipping users with the tools they need to deliver education anywhere, at any scale.

As elearning continues to grow in scope and sophistication, LearnWorlds remains focused on delivering tools that help its users move faster, build smarter, and engage learners more deeply. The company’s upcoming roadmap includes mobile-first microlearning and a significant upgrade to the platform's ecommerce engine.

LearnWorlds will attend Learning Technologies at ExCeL London, 23–24 April 2025, where CEO Panos Siozos will deliver a seminar titled “The $1B Lesson: Why creators outperform L&D departments,” exploring what L&D leaders can learn from the agile creator mindset.

About LearnWorlds

LearnWorlds is a leading elearning platform that helps creators, businesses, and educators deliver branded, engaging learning experiences at scale. Combining LMS functionality with built-in course authoring, ecommerce, mobile learning, and automation tools, LearnWorlds supports over 11,000 customers in 150 countries. Learn more at www.learnworlds.com

About Learning Technologies

Learning Technologies is Europe’s largest L&D event, held annually at ExCeL London. With over 11,000 attendees, 200 exhibitors, and 200 free seminars, it offers a leading forum for exploring innovation, networking, and gaining insight into the future of workplace learning. www.learningtechnologies.co.uk

data.title: Bunzl Shares Hit 4-Year Lows on Guidance Cut, Share-Buyback Pause
data.description:
By Ian Walker

Bunzl shares fell to near-four-year lows after the company cut its guidance for the year and paused its share-buyback program given the uncertain macro environment and resulting margin pressure in its North American business.

Shares were down 770 pence, or 25%, at 23.08 pounds in early morning European Trading. They are currently down 30% over the year to date.

The London-listed distribution-and-outsourcing group said Wednesday that it expects moderate revenue growth this year at constant exchange rates, driven by acquisitions and broadly flat underlying revenue. It said the North American business--which generated about 56% of 2024 revenue--has experienced some revenue softness since its last update in March given the current environment.

Group operating margin for the year is expected to be moderately below 8.0% compared with 8.3% in 2024, Bunzl said.

The company had previously forecast robust revenue growth this year and for its operating margin to be in line with 2024.

Revenue for the year ended Dec. 31 was 11.78 billion pounds ($15.59 billion), of which North America generated 6.57 billion pounds and Continental Europe generated 2.38 billion pounds.

Bunzl said operating margin in the first half of the year is expected to be around 7.0% and be seasonally higher in the second half.

For the first quarter revenue rose 2.6% at constant exchange rates but was up only 0.8% at actual exchange rates. Revenue fell 0.9% on an underlying basis, the company said.

Bunzl said first-quarter adjusted operating profit fell significantly year-on-year given the operating margin decline driven by the North America and Continental Europe businesses.

The company said that it has taken actions to improve its performance in North America, including leadership changes and further cost savings. Margin management and cost initiatives are also expected to boost the company's performance in Continental Europe toward the second half of the year, it said.

"I am disappointed with our performance in the first quarter in this challenging trading environment. We are taking decisive action to improve performance in the group, particularly with regards to execution in our largest business in North America," Chief Executive Frank van Zanten said.

Write to Ian Walker at ian.walker@wsj.com
